prompt: Build a MetroOps Shuttle & Depot Management Portal that lets a private shuttle operator run reservations, service visibility, and depot logistics entirely offline while still supporting rigorous optimization and governance. Dispatchers and station agents use an English web UI rendered with HTMX so arrival boards, route vehicle distribution, and seat availability can refresh in-place without full page reloads; refresh is capped at once every 10 seconds per screen and the UI must clearly show “Last updated at 3:12 PM” plus an “Offline—showing last known data” banner when live updates pause. Riders (kiosk users) can search routes, view next-stop arrival lists, and create bookings that apply pricing and inventory rules such as rate plans by date range, $-based markups/discounts, minimum length of stay equivalents for passes (for example, a 3-day minimum for a commuter bundle), and advance-booking windows (for example, booking must be made at least 2 hours before departure and no more than 30 days out). The reservation flow must hold a seat for 8 minutes during checkout, release it automatically on timeout, and prevent overbooking by enforcing a hard cap of 0 seats remaining (no negative inventory) even under simultaneous agent activity. Supervisors can run A/B experiments on recommendation widgets (for example, “Suggested departure times” or “Alternative routes”) with 50/50 traffic splitting and version labels visible in the UI, while operations analysts view dashboards for CTR, conversion-to-booking, and 7-day return usage alongside offline ranking metrics such as Precision, Recall, NDCG, Coverage, and Diversity. Employees also maintain structured Learning Records and Notes with Markdown, file attachments up to 20 MB, bidirectional links between incidents and training tasks, cross-task rollups, and one-click rollback to any of the last 20 versions. Internal social features support like/favorite, follow, block, and report, with a relationship state where mutual follow can unlock extra profile fields such as role and depot assignment. The backend uses Flask to implement offline-first business services and REST-style APIs consumed by HTMX, with SQLite as the single local system of record for reservations, rule configurations, vehicle pings, warehouse hierarchy, experiments, notes, and relationship states. Vehicle location and ETA are ingested only from local sources such as a dispatcher upload (CSV) or a LAN-connected GPS gateway; when no new pings arrive for 2 minutes, ETAs must fall back to the last known schedule and the system must flag anomalies like impossible speed jumps (for example, >85 mph between consecutive pings) and excessive refresh attempts (more than 30 per minute per user) as risk events. Depot inventory is modeled hierarchically as warehouse/zone/bin with configurable bin types and capacity rules (volume in cubic feet and load in pounds), availability statuses, and freeze/unfreeze controls that immediately block allocations while preserving counts. Security is enforced through RBAC (employee/supervisor/HR/admin) with fine-grained permissions on notes, reports, and experiment controls; authentication supports local username/password only with minimum 12 characters and lockout for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts. Sessions expire after 30 minutes of inactivity, JWT or server sessions may be used, and all traffic must use TLS even on internal networks. Sensitive fields are encrypted at rest, face-related data (if stored as an internal identifier) must be minimized, never displayed in full, and always masked in logs; replay and tampering controls include nonce-based request validation on high-risk actions such as booking confirmation and inventory adjustments.
